methodsAttempted.query: site:iisc.ac.in Indian Institute of Science generative AI policy students assessment teaching ChatGPT
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found two official IISc PDFs on emerging AI tools for education and research, plus course and research records outside the central policy scope.
Search found the same official committee report and faculty presentation; no current official handbook page confirming adoption of the recommended wording was found.
The official PDFs were fetched and parsed with Firecrawl PDF extraction, then direct HTTP HEAD and local text hashes were used to record fetch metadata.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found an IISc committee report, dated January 10, 2024, and an IISc faculty presentation on emerging AI tools for education and research. The report is framed as analysis and recommendations, including proposed institute-level baseline policies and research guidance. No current official Student Handbook source confirming formal adoption was found, so claims are scoped to the report or presentation rather than stated as binding current handbook policy. One official course page was rejected for broad university claims because it is course-specific.

Do not infer university-wide policy from course-specific pages.

artifacts.claimText: An IISc committee report recommends 'allowed with attribution' as the default institutional policy for AI tool use in courses, while allowing instructors, departments, divisions, or programmes to tailor the baseline policy.

artifacts.claimText: IISc faculty-facing AI guidance says entering data into an open AI tool should be treated like posting it publicly unless there are strong reasons to trust the tool, and says not to input sensitive, confidential, or restricted information into open AI tools.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Emerging AI Tools for Education and Research: Perspective and Policies for IISc
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: We recommend allowed with attribution as the default institutional policy. This default policy should be articulated via an appropriate channel, such as the Student Handbook. Furthermore, this default policy can be tailored at each level in the hierarchy: individual course instructor, department, division, or programme.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The committee recommends an allowed-with-attribution default for coursework AI use, with tailoring by instructors or academic units.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Section 5.3, Transparent and Simple Course Policies

artifacts.sourceTitle: Emerging AI Tools for Education and Research: Perspective and Policies for IISc
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Use of generative AI should be fully disclosed, with attribution that is precise, transparent, and with adequate details. In particular, when one is using generative AI in publications, one should follow the guidelines of the Journal, Conference, or Publisher. For thesis and other Institute documents, one should follow the guidelines for publications given below.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The report recommends detailed disclosure and attribution for generative AI use in publications, theses, and other Institute documents.

artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Hence, entering data into an AI tool should be regarded as equivalent to posting it in public (unless there are strong reasons to trust that a specific tool will not use your queries). Do not enter, contribute, or otherwise input sensitive, confidential, or restricted information into open AI tools.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The faculty presentation warns against entering sensitive, confidential, or restricted information into open AI tools.

artifacts.title: Indian Institute of Science AI Policy Evidence Draft
artifacts.summary: Official IISc sources support narrow staged claims that a committee report recommends allowed-with-attribution as the default course AI policy baseline, recommends detailed disclosure and attribution for generative AI use in research and Institute documents, and warns against entering sensitive, confidential, or restricted information into open AI tools. The crawl did not find a current official source confirming formal Student Handbook adoption, and a course-specific AI rule was rejected for broad university claims.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
The main evidence is a committee report and faculty presentation; claims are not framed as formally adopted handbook language.
Course-specific evidence was rejected for broad university-wide claims.
